input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A building asset in the architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain. Its BIM model has been enriched with device annotations—sensors, actuators and controllers—to bridge the gap between static BIM representations and the dynamic behaviour required for digital‑twin validation. The study focuses on this building’s integrated infrastructure, enabling real‑time simulation, monitoring and control through its digital twin counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Real‑time data exchange that synchronises sensor and actuator states between the physical building and its digital twin, providing up‑to‑date operational insights. Continuous monitoring of building parameters via embedded sensors, exposing key metrics through the twin. Command and control services that allow facility managers to adjust actuator settings through the twin’s control logic. Interactive visualization giving immersive views of the building’s state and control interfaces. Extensible plugin support that lets DT engineers add custom analytics or logic layers on top of the core twin.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models, with automated generation of deployable Digital Twin artifacts. Device modeling capability addresses the limitation of BIM in representing BCS components by allowing Building Control Engineers to define sensors, actuators and controllers within the BIM environment. The modular architecture exposes standardized extension points for custom services, enabling integration with analytics and simulation engines. Open‑source digital twin frameworks referenced for potential enablers include Eclipse BAcyx and itwin.js, which provide platform layers for industrial and infrastructure digital twin applications.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real‑time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between the physical building and its digital twin, but the documents do not specify exact synchronization frequencies, latency requirements or temporal granularity. The architecture is designed to support real‑time updates and continuous convergence between the physical and virtual domains.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The documents do not describe multiple Digital Twin instances, a hierarchical or federated structure, or coordination mechanisms between twins. The focus is on a single DT per building or asset, with no explicit mention of centralized vs decentralized deployment or scope of responsibilities per instance.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a model‑driven engineering architecture pattern: enriched BIM models are transformed into structured representations, from which deployable DT artifacts are automatically generated. The modular architecture comprises a central hub, component‑level device twins, custom service plug‑ins, and analytics modules. Resource management is handled through runtime configuration management, enabling dynamic scaling and low‑overhead updates, while system‑wide coordination relies on bidirectional data exchange and standardized integration points.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability across heterogeneous systems is addressed through non‑functional requirements NF_X, providing integration with external enterprise systems, BIM tools, sensor networks, and facility management platforms. The framework exposes standardized extension points and modular components that facilitate data exchange with other digital twins and enterprise systems, leveraging common integration patterns and open data protocols (details not specified in the documents).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin ecosystem relies on enriched BIM (Revit/IFC) models that embed geometric building representations together with device metadata (e.g., sensor, actuator, controller definitions). During deployment the framework performs model checking (≈5.53 s) and parsing (≈8.7 s) before initializing a deployable artifact in 0.3 s. Enriched BIM is transformed into a set of TwinComponents and FeatureParsers that encode behavioral logic; for example, the LightTwinComponent encapsulates an LED fixture and maps its photometric metadata (Revit light power, colour temperature, distribution) onto a UE 5 light. The resulting device registry is managed by Eclipse Ditto, which supplies a policy‑based validation engine that enforces value ranges, units and compatibility checks on sensor data and actuator commands, and persists all state changes with at‑least‑once message delivery via Eclipse Hono. The hierarchy—geometric BIM → validation → conversion & configuration → middleware (Hono / Ditto) → UE 5 visualization—provides a unified, semantic foundation for real‑time synchronization and interactive operation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The twin’s actionable layer consists primarily of lighting actuators represented by the LightTwinComponent. Each component is identified by a unique device ID assigned during BIM modeling and exposes control parameters such as power state (on/off), brightness (0–100 % or lumens), and colour‑temperature (≈2 500 – 6 500 K). Actuation commands are transmitted from the Twin via the UnrealTwinController to Ditto, which routes them through Hono with MQTT‑style secure transport. Before forwarding, Ditto applies pre‑execution policy checks that validate the command against defined limits and refuse out‑of‑range values, thereby enforcing safety constraints that prevent hardware damage or unsafe operational states. The Building Control Engineer defines the control logic in the BIM model; the Facility Manager can adjust lighting states in real time through the Twin’s visualization interface, which reflects changes from the physical device immediately thanks to Hono’s at‑least‑once delivery.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The framework implements rigorous fidelity and validity checks at multiple layers. Device and state data are validated by Ditto’s policy engine, which enforces unit consistency and permissible value ranges; any violation triggers an exception and blocks the affected command. Eclipse Hono guarantees at‑least‑once delivery, while Ditto’s persistent event store ensures availability of state history for rollback and auditing. Model integrity is verified during the Model Checking phase, where structural constraints of BIM and TwinComponents are evaluated before deployment. Operational performance is quantified using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric, whose components—timeliness (0.997), reliability (1.0), and availability (1.0)—are measured over a 12‑minute window with a 1‑second heartbeat. The resulting ODTE score (0.997) confirms that the twin remains tightly coupled to its physical counterpart, with negligible lag and no message loss. In case of synchronous failures, the system’s fault‑tolerant design—using event replay and state restoration—maintains continuity and preserves model validity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor and actuator data (e.g., temperature, humidity, power consumption, occupancy, equipment status) is captured by on‑site gateways and streamed to the Digital Twin via Eclipse Hono, which acts as the MQTT‑based messaging broker. The broker forwards the payloads to Eclipse Ditto, where the twin’s JSON state is updated through Ditto’s REST/CoAP APIs. This integration delivers near‑real‑time synchronization with low latency and is triggered by either sensor value changes or periodic polling (e.g., on change events or simple interval timers). Each incoming message is validated against a predefined JSON Schema, enriched with metadata such as source identifier, timestamp, and geographic coordinates, and merged into Ditto’s state store using Ditto’s incremental update semantics. The result is a continuously updated, semantically‑consistent digital representation of the physical building.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the Digital Twin (e.g., HVAC setpoints, lighting levels, actuator activations) are emitted as JSON payloads via Ditto’s command interface. These messages are routed through Eclipse Hono to the device gateways, which translate them into the device‑specific protocol used by the Building Control System (e.g., BACnet, Modbus, or OPC UA). Command messages are validated against a schema, authenticated through Ditto’s security module (using OAuth 2.0 or JWT tokens signed by the tenant’s authority), and authorised according to the user role (Facility Manager or DT Engineer). Feedback is collected by the physical devices and sent back through the same channel as telemetry updates, which overwrite the twin’s “status” or “execution_state” properties to confirm command execution or report failures. In cases of conflicting commands, a central conflict‑resolution service applies predefined priority rules (e.g., manual override has precedence) and logs the resolution. Execution confirmations are explicitly acknowledged by the gateway sending an ACK message that updates the twin’s state.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin framework relies on Eclipse Hono for robust, scalable messaging between physical devices and the twin platform. Hono exposes MQTT for device clients and HTTP/AMQP for broker services, enabling secure, asynchronous communication. Messages are routed by tenant ID to dedicated instances of Eclipse Ditto, which maintains twin state via REST and AMQP APIs. The network topology is a hybrid star: devices connect to local gateways that forward data through Hono to a clustered broker, ensuring persistence, QoS 1/2, and fail‑over. Bandwidth requirements remain modest per sensor (<10 kB/s) but aggregate metrics scale with asset count. Latency targets are sub‑200 ms to satisfy real‑time control loops. Reliability is achieved through Hono’s persistent sessions, broker clustering, and Ditto’s state replication to persistent storage (e.g., PostgreSQL). Redundancy is provided by deploying dual Hono brokers and local edge gateways to act independently in case of broker outage. All communication layers enforce TLS 1.2+ encryption; devices authenticate with X.509 certificates or JWTs, and server‑to‑server interactions use OAuth 2.0 for secure credential exchange.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle follows a model‑driven sequence beginning with **Design** where Building Control Engineers enrich BIM models with device, sensor, and controller definitions. It continues to **Model‑to‑Artifact Transformation** employing Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to extract structured representations and generate deployable DT artifacts. The next phase, **Automated Deployment**, is governed by requirement C2 and reduces manual configuration, enabling reproducible DT setups. During **Runtime Operation**, real‑time data integration via Eclipse Hono and Ditto (C3) ensures continuous synchronization and interactive visualization, supporting Facility Managers in monitoring and control. Finally, **Evolution & Extensibility** are facilitated by a modular, service‑oriented architecture with standardized extension points (C4), allowing DT Engineers to inject custom services and plugins while maintaining version consistency through BIM model revisions and DT artifact repositories.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The framework adopts a model‑driven engineering approach rooted in MDE, allowing devices to be defined directly in BIM. Enriched models are automatically transformed into structured representations, and deployable DT artifacts are generated with minimal manual intervention, satisfying requirement C2. Runtime data exchanges via Eclipse Hono and Ditto (C3) provide continuous state consistency and serve as a validation layer. The modular, service‑oriented architecture (C4) offers defined extension points that enable DT Engineers to develop and integrate custom services and analytics, supporting evolution. Versioning is implicitly managed through BIM model revisions and corresponding DT artifact repositories, although explicit version control mechanisms are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The digital twin delivers real‑time monitoring, predictive maintenance, and operational optimization insights for building assets. Live data streams from sensors are ingested via Eclipse Hono, aggregated, and visualized through immersive 3‑D dashboards that allow Facility Managers to interactively query state, simulate future scenarios, and adjust control parameters. Simulation and analytical modules (implied by the framework’s support for custom services) enable scenario testing and life‑cycle‑encompassing optimization. Decision support is thus grounded in continuous, data‑driven analysis, though explicit confidence metrics or recommendation engines are not defined in the documentation.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the physical system as a building asset in the AECO domain. It correctly identifies the enrichment of BIM models with device annotations (sensors, actuators, controllers) to transition from static to dynamic representations, and highlights the study's focus on real-time simulation and control. This aligns perfectly with the paper's definition of the system and its transformation.

The extracted services are explicitly supported by the text. Real-time data exchange corresponds to the integration of Eclipse Hono and Ditto; visualization corresponds to Unreal Engine; command and control correspond to the Facility Manager's role; and extensibility corresponds to the standardized extension points and DT Engineer's role.

The extraction accurately describes the reliance on 'Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)' and the 'automated generation' of artifacts. It correctly identifies the 'device modeling capability' within the 'BIM environment' to address BIM limitations regarding 'sensors, actuators, and controllers'. It also correctly references 'Eclipse BaSyx' (noted as Eclipse BAcyx) and 'itwin.js' as frameworks discussed in the context of open-source tools. While it omits the specific implementation tools used in the proposed solution (Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine), the details provided regarding the methodology and referenced frameworks are strongly grounded in the text.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies that the system supports real-time synchronization but accurately notes the absence of specific technical details like frequencies or latency. Supported by quotes: 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'.

The extraction correctly identifies that the documents do not describe multiple instances, hierarchical structures, or coordination mechanisms between twins, focusing instead on a single representation of an asset. Supported by quotes: 'virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building)' and the lack of mention regarding multiple instances in the text.

This extraction is highly detailed and strongly grounded in the source text. It accurately describes the 'model-driven engineering' pattern ('model-driven framework'), the transformation of 'enriched BIM models' into 'deployable DT artifacts' ('automates the extraction... generation of runtime interfaces'), and the modular architecture featuring a 'central hub', 'custom service plug-ins' ('standardized extension points'), and 'runtime configuration management'.

The extraction correctly cites 'non-functional requirements NF_X' and 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems' to describe integration capabilities. It accurately identifies 'standardized extension points' as the mechanism for this. While it lists specific external systems (BIM tools, sensor networks) which are contextually present but not explicitly listed as integration targets in a single sentence, the description remains specific and accurate. The extraction also correctly notes that 'open data protocols' details are not specified in the documents.

Fig. 2: Component and connector view of our model-driven DT deployment framework. The DT Framework processes
enriched BIM models and device configurations through validation, conversion, and configuration components. These feed
into middleware (Hono and Ditto) and visualization services (UE Engine), enabling bidirectional integration with the physical

and updates the UE5 scene rendering accordingly. These
programmatic extension points (cf. TwinComponent and
FeatureParser) allow for extending our proposal with additional, custom DT services inside UE5. Observe that
HVACFeatureParser and HVACTwinComponent in Figure 3 are
only for illustrative purposes and currently not implemented in
our proposal. Table IV provides a concise overview on these
extension points.
Our framework ensures data integrity through Eclipse
Ditto’s policy-based validation, which enforces value ranges
and units for sensor data, and pre-execution checks for actuator commands to prevent conflicts or unsafe operations.
Fault tolerance is provided by Eclipse Hono’s at-least-once
message delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, ensuring resilience against network failures or service disruptions.
These mechanisms collectively maintain synchronization and
operational safety between physical assets and their DTs.


V. R ESULTS AND D ISCUSSION

We have evaluated our approach in the context of a Living
Lab [75] at the University of Innsbruck. Figure 4a shows the
BIM model of the Living Lab, which comprises an office space

turn manages the devices (cf. DittoDevice) and associated connections (cf. DittoConnection). Technically, the

Fig. 2: Component and connector view of our model-driven DT deployment framework. The DT Framework processes
enriched BIM models and device configurations through validation, conversion, and configuration components. These feed

and updates the UE5 scene rendering accordingly. These

Our framework ensures data integrity through Eclipse
Ditto’s policy-based validation, which enforces value ranges
and units for sensor data, and pre-execution checks for actuator commands to prevent conflicts or unsafe operations.
Fault tolerance is provided by Eclipse Hono’s at-least-once
message delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, ensuring resilience against network failures or service disruptions.
These mechanisms collectively maintain synchronization and
operational safety between physical assets and their DTs.


V. R ESULTS AND D ISCUSSION

We have evaluated our approach in the context of a Living
Lab [75] at the University of Innsbruck. Figure 4a shows the
BIM model of the Living Lab, which comprises an office space
of 22 m [2], two working places, as well as various sensors and

Fig. 4: Visualization of the Living Lab: (a) BIM model in Revit (b) 3D rendering in UE5.



TABLE VI: Deployment Times for Digital Twin


**Stage** **Duration** (seconds)


Model Checking 5.53
Model Parsing 8.7
Initialization and Setup 0.3


in 0.3 seconds. This considerably short deployment time of
14.53 seconds underscores the efficiency of our proposal in
streamlining the DT creation process. Our deployment was
done on a standard

The Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) [18] metric is a crucial measure of the synchronization quality between DTs and their physical counterparts. It is composed of
three main components: timeliness, reliability, and availability.
_Timeliness_ ( _T_ ) reflects how up-to-date the data is that the
DT receives, measuring the delay between a state change
occurring in the physical counterpart and the subsequent
update in the DT. _Reliability_ ( _R_ ) measures the consistency
in communication between the physical counterpart and the
DT, defined as the ratio of the number of state updates
successfully received by the DT to the number of updates
expected. _Availability_ ( _A_ ) assesses the operational uptime of
the physical counterpart as perceived by the DT, measuring
the extent to which the physical counterpart is accessible and
capable of sending updates. The ODTE score then is calculated
as the product of these components (cf. _ODTE_ = _T ×R×A_ ),
providing a comprehensive assessment of system performance.
Table VII shows our results.

In our assessment, the scores were 0.997 for timeliness
(indicating some slight delay in communication), 1.0 for
reliability (no message loss), and 1.0 for availability (100%
up-time) resulting in an overall ODTE value of 0.997. We
measured the ODTE metric over a continuous 12-minute



TABLE VII: ODTE Measurements


**ODTE Component** **Score**


Timeliness 0.997
Reliability 1.0
Availability 1.0


**Total Score** 0.997


measurement window. The setup employed a regular 1-second
heartbeat mechanism to coordinate data exchange and state
updates between the physical and digital twin. Throughout
the experiment, the DT maintained a consistent and exact
reflection of the physical system’s state, with no observable
divergence in state, reflecting ideal entanglement under the
given conditions. Our experiments assess bidirectional synchronization by transmitting lighting control commands from
the DT to the physical system and verifying the corresponding
state updates in the reverse direction. The absence of such
updates would indicate a communication failure, resulting in
a total ODTE score of 0, thereby validating the integrity of
the bidirectional communication channel.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

[64]. Requirements regarding the complexity of construction
projects and availability over long building life cycles are
covered by BIM. It is therefore essential that concepts such as
DTs in the construction sector are also designed for longevity
and compatibility with existing, AECO-specific systems and
standards such as BIM.
Despite these strengths, BIM however exhibits significant
limitations when considering its application as a foundation
for DTs [7], [9], [14]. First, BIM models are predominantly
static representations focused on design and construction
phases, lacking mechanisms for real-time data integration from
operational buildings. Second, BIM tools typically provide
limited support for bidirectional communication with physical
systems, a core requirement for functional DTs. Third, while
BIM excels at representing physical and spatial aspects of
buildings, it lacks native capabilities for modeling runtime
aspects of Building Control Systems (BCS), essential for
operational monitoring and control [7], [11], [12], [21].
These limitations create a fundamental gap between BIM’s
design-time focus and the operational requirements of DTs.
DTs require continuous synchronization with physical assets
through sensor data, the ability to process and analyze this
data in real-time, and mechanisms to feed control signals
back to the BCS. In addition, DTs also require extensible
architectures that can accommodate evolving services and
analytics throughout the building life cycle [7].
Nevertheless, BIM provides an ideal semantic foundation
for DTs due to its comprehensive representation of building


geometry, topology, and component relationships. What is
needed is a systematic approach to transform these static
BIM models into dynamic, operational DTs by addressing the
identified limitations and extending BIM through a formal
methodology for modeling BCS runtime aspects and their
integration with a building’s digital representation.


_A. Uses Cases, Requirements, and Solution Proposal_


TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
